name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱلذَّٰرِيَـٰتِ ذَرْوًا (١)
```

```english
By the scattering [winds] that scatter [the clouds]; (1)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْحَـٰمِلَـٰتِ وِقْرًا (٢)
```

```english
by the [rain] bearing [clouds] laden [with water]; (2)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْجَـٰرِيَـٰتِ يُسْرًا (٣)
```

```english
by [the ships] which move gently [on the sea]; (3)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْمُقَسِّمَـٰتِ أَمْرًا (٤)
```

```english
by [the angels] who dispense [livelihood] by [His] command: (4)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَصَادِقٌ (٥)
```

```english
indeed what you are promised is true, (5)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ لَوَٰقِعٌ (٦)
```

```english
and indeed the retribution will surely come to pass! (6)
```

```arabic
وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلْحُبُكِ (٧)
```

```english
By the sky full of adornment [with stars], (7)
```

```arabic
إِنَّكُمْ لَفِى قَوْلٍ مُّخْتَلِفٍ (٨)
```

```english
indeed you are of different opinions! (8)
```

```arabic
يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ (٩)
```

```english
He who has been turned away [from the truth] is turned away from it. (9)
```

```arabic
قُتِلَ ٱلْخَرَّٰصُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
Perish the liars, (10)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى غَمْرَةٍ سَاهُونَ (١١)
```

```english
who are heedless in a stupor! (11)
```

```arabic
يَسْـَٔلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (١٢)
```

```english
They ask, ‘When will be the Day of Retribution?’ (12)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ (١٣)
```

```english
It is the day when they will be tormented in the Fire, (13)
```

```arabic
ذُوقُوا۟ فِتْنَتَكُمْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
[and will be told]: ‘Taste your torment. This is what you used to hasten.’ (14)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (١٥)
```

```english
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and springs, (15)
```

```arabic
ءَاخِذِينَ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَبْلَ ذَٰلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ (١٦)
```

```english
receiving what their Lord has given them, for they had been virtuous aforetime. (16)
```

```arabic
كَانُوا۟ قَلِيلًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مَا يَهْجَعُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
They used to sleep a little during the night, (17)
```

```arabic
وَبِٱلْأَسْحَارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (١٨)
```

```english
and at dawns they would plead for forgiveness, (18)
```

```arabic
وَفِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِّلسَّآئِلِ وَٱلْمَحْرُومِ (١٩)
```

```english
and there was a share in their wealth for the beggar and the deprived. (19)
```

## (Commentary)

The prophetic mission (الدعوة النبوية) used to call the pagans to the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — *the belief that Allah alone is the Sustainer and Governor of all affairs* — and to the fact that Allah the Exalted is their Lord and the Lord of all things. This call was conducted through the means of warning (الإنذار) and glad tidings (التبشير), specifically emphasizing warning.

The warning was of Allah’s punishment in this world for the deniers—the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال)—and in the Hereafter with eternal punishment on the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة). This latter point is the primary pillar for the success of the mission; for if it were not for the reckoning (الحساب) and the recompense (الجزاء) on the Day of Resurrection, faith in Divine Oneness (الوحدانية) and Prophethood (النبوة) would be futile (لغو) and without effect.

By taking gods besides Allah, the glorified, the polytheists intensified their denial of the foundations of monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood, and the return (المعاد) — *the restoration of life after death for judgment*. They were obstinate in denying the Return, persistent in its negation, and mocking it through every possible means, as they perceived that its invalidation would lead to the invalidation of the other two foundations.

This Surah mentions the Return and their denial of it; it begins with it and concludes upon it. However, it does not address it in terms of its own essence, as the discourse proceeds in other places of His speech, but rather in terms of it being the Day of Recompense (يوم الجزاء) and that Allah, who promised it to them, is their Lord; He is the one who promised it, and His promise is a truth (صدق) in which there is no doubt.

Therefore, when the discourse transitions to arguing for it, it employs the proofs of monotheism from the signs (آيات) of the earth, the heavens, and the souls (الأنفس), as well as the punishments Allah inflicted upon past nations following their call to monotheism and their denial of His messengers. This is solely to establish monotheism through them, thereby establishing the Day of Recompense which Allah promised—and Allah does not fail His promise—and which the prophetic mission announced. Thus, their denial of the recompense is refuted, a denial they used as a means to invalidate the religion of monotheism and the message of the Messenger, since faith in it would otherwise become futile and without effect, as previously indicated.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses, and there is no disagreement on this. Among its most luminous verses is His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ** — "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me." (51:56).

The section of verses we have presented is the opening of the discourse, in which it is mentioned that the recompense they are promised is a truth, and that their denial and obstinacy regarding it is mere conjecture (تخرص). It then describes the Day of Recompense and the state of the God-conscious (المتقين) and the deniers therein.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً** — "By the [winds] that scatter [the dust] far and wide, by the [clouds] that bear the burden [of rain], by the [ships] that sail with ease, by the [angels] who dispense [the provisions] by [His] command," (51:1-4). **الذاريات** (the scatterers) is the plural of *al-dhāriyah*, from the expression: "the wind scattered (ذرت) the dust" when it makes it fly. *Al-wiqr* (الوقر), with a *kasrah* on the first letter and *sukūn* on the second, refers to a heavy load carried on the back or in the womb.

In these verses, there is oath after oath, providing emphasis after emphasis for the subject of the oath, which is the recompense for deeds. His saying: **وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً** is an oath by the winds that stir up the dust. His saying: **فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً**, with the particle *fā’* (ف) denoting sequence and order, is conjoined to "the scatterers" and is an oath by the clouds carrying the heavy weight of water. His saying: **فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً** is conjoined to it and is an oath by the ships sailing in the seas with ease and facility.

His saying: **فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً** is conjoined to what preceded it and is an oath by the angels (الملائكة) who act by His command, distributing it according to their varying stations. For the command (الأمر) of the Possessor of the Throne regarding creation and governance is one; but when a group of angels carries it out according to their diverse tasks, the command branches out and is divided by their division. Then, if a group lower than the first group carries it, it is divided a second time by their division, and so on, until it reaches the angels directly responsible for particular cosmic events (الحوادث الكونية الجزئية), where it divides by their division and multiplies by their multiplication.

The four verses—as you see—point to the totality of governance (عامة التدبير), as they mention a model of how affairs are governed on land, which is "the scatterers far and wide"; a model of how affairs are governed at sea, which is "those that sail with ease"; and a model of how affairs are governed in the atmosphere, which is "those that bear the burden." All of this is completed by the angels, who are the intermediaries of governance (وسائط التدبير), and they are "those who dispense the command."

Thus, the verses carry the meaning of saying: I swear by all the causes (الأسباب) through which the matter of governance in the world is completed, that such-and-forth is true. It has been narrated through both Shi'a and Sunni chains from Ali, upon whom be the best of peace (عليه أفضل السلام), that he interpreted these four verses as mentioned above.

It is reported from Fakhr al-Razi in *al-Tafsir al-Kabir* that it is more likely that all four verses refer to the winds, for they scatter the dust, carry the heavy clouds, move through the air with ease, and distribute the clouds over the regions of the earth.

The truth is that what he considered likely is far-fetched, and the meaning previously mentioned is more eloquent than what he stated.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَصادِقٌ وَإِنَّ الدِّينَ لَواقِعٌ** — "Indeed what you are promised is true, and indeed the Retribution will surely take place." (51:5-6). The word *mā* (ما) is a relative pronoun, and the pronoun referring back to it is omitted, meaning "that which you are promised." Or it is an infinitive particle (مصدرية), and **تُوعَدُونَ** (you are promised) is from "the promise" (الوعد), as supported by His saying: **وَإِنَّ الدِّينَ لَواقِعٌ**, which encompasses absolute recompense. It is also said to be from "the threat" (الإيعاد), as supported by His saying: **فَذَكِّرْ بِالْقُرْآنِ مَنْ يَخافُ وَعِيدِ** — "So admonish by the Quran him who fears My threat." (50:45).

Describing the promise as "truthful" (صادق) is a metaphorical attribution (مجاز في النسبة), as in His saying: **فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ** — "in a pleasant life" (69:21), or "truthful" means "possessing truth," as has been said regarding the same verse. **الدين** (the Religion) here means the recompense (الجزاء).

In any case, His saying: **إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَصادِقٌ** is the response to the oath (جواب القسم), and His saying: **وَإِنَّ الدِّينَ لَواقِعٌ** is conjoined to it, serving as an explanation. The meaning is: I swear by such-and-forth that what you are promised—which is what the Quran or the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) promises them by what was revealed to him—regarding the Day of Resurrection and that Allah will recompense them therein for their deeds, if good then good, and if evil then evil, is indeed true, and the recompense will surely occur.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالسَّماءِ ذاتِ الْحُبُكِ** — "By the sky with its [starry] tracks," (51:7). **الحبك** (the tracks) means beauty and adornment, and it also means a well-proportioned creation. It can also be the plural of *habīkah* or *hibāk*, meaning a path (الطريقة), like the paths that appear on water when it ripples and breaks from the wind passing over it.

According to the first meaning, the sense is: I swear by the heaven possessing beauty and adornment, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِزِينَةٍ الْكَواكِبِ** — "Indeed We have adorned the lowest heaven with the adornment of the stars," (37:6). According to the second: I swear by the heaven possessing a well-proportioned creation, similar to His saying: **وَالسَّماءَ بَنَيْناها بِأَيْدٍ** — "We have built the sky with might," (51:47). According to the third: I swear by the heaven possessing paths, similar to His saying: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرائِقَ** — "Certainly We have created above you seven paths," (23:17).

Perhaps the third meaning is the most apparent due to its suitability to the response of the oath, which is the disagreement of the people and the scattering of their paths, just as the previous oaths: "By the scatterers far and wide," etc., shared the meaning of flowing and traveling, suitable to their response: "Indeed what you are promised," etc., which contains the meaning of returning to Allah and traveling toward Him.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي قَوْلٍ مُخْتَلِفٍ يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ** — "you are surely in a discordant view. He who is perverted is perverted from it." (51:8-9). Discordant view (القول المختلف) is that which is contradictory and refutes itself. Since the discourse is about establishing the truthfulness of the Quran, the mission, or the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding what he promised them of the Resurrection and Recompense, the "discordant view"—most likely—refers to their differing statements regarding the Quran for the purpose of denying what it establishes. Sometimes they say: it is sorcery and the one who brought it is a sorcerer; sometimes they say: it is a trance and the one who brought it is possessed; sometimes they say: it is the suggestion of devils and the one who brought it is a soothsayer; sometimes they say: it is poetry and the one who brought it is a poet; sometimes that it is a fabrication; sometimes they say: only a human teaches him; and sometimes they say: fables of the ancients which he has written down.

His saying: **يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ**. *Al-ifk* (الإفك) means turning away (الصرف). The pronoun in "from it" (عَنْهُ) refers to the Book in terms of its inclusion of the promise of Resurrection and Recompense. The meaning is: he who is turned away is turned away from the Quran. It is also said that the pronoun refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), meaning: he who is turned away is turned away from believing in him. You have seen that the previous meaning is more in accord with the context, even if the ultimate result of both meanings is the same.

It is narrated from some that the pronoun in "from it" refers to "what you are promised" or to "the Religion." Allah first swore by the scatterers and others that the Resurrection and Recompense are true, then He swore by the heaven that they are in a discordant view regarding its occurrence—some being doubters and others deniers. Then He said: he who is perverted is perverted from acknowledging the matter of Resurrection and Recompense. This view is close to the previous one.

From others: the pronoun refers to "discordant view," and the particle "from" (عن) denotes causality, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَما نَحْنُ بِتارِكِي آلِهَتِنا عَنْ قَوْلِكَ** — "we are not going to abandon our gods for your saying," (11:53). Thus, the sentence would be an attribute of "view," and the meaning: you are in a discordant view because of which he who is perverted is perverted. This is a fine view.

It is also said: the pronoun in "you" (إِنَّكُمْ) refers to both the Muslim and the disbeliever, so the discordant view would mean the view of the Muslims regarding the occurrence of the Resurrection and Recompense, and the view of the disbelievers regarding its non-occurrence. Perhaps the context does not suit this. Other weak views have been mentioned which are of no use to address.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُتِلَ الْخَرَّاصُونَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ ساهُونَ يَسْئَلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الدِّينِ** — "Perish the liars! —those who are in a stupor of heedlessness. They ask, ‘When is the Day of Retribution?’" (51:10-12). The root of *al-kharṣ* (الخرص) is speaking by conjecture and guesswork without knowledge. Because speaking without knowledge carries the risk of lying, the liar is called a *kharrāṣ* (conjecturer). It is most likely that the "conjecturers" in the verse refers to those who speak without knowledge or proof, those who plunge into the matter of Resurrection and Recompense, denying it without knowledge.

In His saying: **قُتِلَ الْخَرَّاصُونَ**, there is an invocation against them for death, which is a metaphor for a type of expulsion and deprivation from success; this is what the interpretation of those who explained it as "cursing" (اللعن) boils down to.

His saying: **الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ ساهُونَ**. *Al-ghamrah* (الغمرة)—as mentioned by al-Raghib—is the mass of water that covers its bed, and it is used as a metaphor for the ignorance that overwhelms its possessor. *Al-sahw* (السهو) is said to mean absolute heedlessness (الغفلة).

The meaning of the verse as it describes the conjecturers is: those who are in an ignorance that has surrounded them, heedless of the reality of what they have been informed of.

His saying: **يَسْئَلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الدِّينِ**. The plural pronoun refers to the conjecturers; it is a statement they made by way of seeking haste in mockery, like their saying: **مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — "When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?" (36:48).

The question using *ayyāna* (أَيَّانَ)—which is designated for asking about the time of its object—regarding the Day of Recompense, which is clearly a time itself, is only by way of considering that the Day of Recompense, being promised, is treated like temporal events. Thus, it is asked about as temporal events are asked about with *ayyāna* and *matā* (when), just as it is said: "When is the day of the festival?" because it is an event treated as a temporal thing. So it has been said.

It is possible that it is from the expansion (التوسع) of the meaning of adverbiality (الظرفية), by considering the specific attributes of the adverb as an adverb itself by way of expansion. Thus, the question about the time of a time is a question of whether it is after which time or before which time? As it is said: "When is the day of the festival?" and the answer is that it is after ten days, for example, or before such-and-forth day. This is an expansion common in custom, not exclusive to the speech of the Arabs, and there is much of it in the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ** — "The day when they are tormented in the Fire." (51:13). The plural pronoun refers to the conjecturers. *Al-fatn* (الفتن) originally means putting gold into the fire to manifest its quality, then it was used for absolute burning and punishment. The adverbial phrase is related to an omitted verb or is a subject (مبتدأ). The verse is an answer to their question, in which it diverts from explaining the specific time of the Day of Recompense to explaining its description and pointing to their state therein, because its time is from the Unseen (الغيب) which none knows but Allah. He said: **لا يُجَلِّيها لِوَقْتِها إِلَّا هُوَ** — "None shall manifest it at its time but He." (7:187).

The estimation and meaning of the verse is: the Day of Recompense occurs, or it is happening, on the day when they—the conjecturers—are punished or burned in the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذُوقُوا فِتْنَتَكُمْ هذَا الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ** — "‘Taste your punishment! This is what you used to seek to hasten.’" (51:14). This is a narration of a speech from Him, the Exalted, or from the angels by His command to the conjecturers while they are being tormented in the Fire on that day.

The meaning is: it is said to them, "Taste the punishment that is specific to you. This punishment is what you used to seek to hasten when you said in haste and mockery: 'When is the Day of Recompense?'"

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ** — "Indeed the God-conscious will be amid gardens and springs," (51:15). This is an explanation of the state of the God-conscious (المتقين) — *those who are wary of Allah's boundaries and protect themselves from His punishment* — on the Day of Recompense, after describing the state of those conjecturers.

The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "gardens" and "springs" is to point to the greatness of their status, as if they are such that describers cannot describe them. The springs have been conjoined to the gardens in their adverbiality by way of expansion.

His saying, the Exalted: **آخِذِينَ ما آتاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَبْلَ ذلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ** — "receiving what their Lord has given them. Indeed they were virtuous before that." (51:16). That is, accepting what their Lord, who is Compassionate toward them, has given them, being pleased with Him and with what He has given, as suggested by the specific expression of "receiving" (الأخذ) and "giving" (الإيتاء) and the attribution of giving to their Lord.

His saying: **إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَبْلَ ذلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ** is a justification for what preceded it; i.e., their state is such because they were before that—meaning in the world—possessors of excellence (إحسان) in their deeds, i.e., possessors of righteous deeds (أعمال حسنة).

His saying, the Exalted: **كانُوا قَلِيلاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ ما يَهْجَعُونَ** — "They used to sleep but little of the night," (51:17). These verses are an explanation of their excellence. *Al-hajū‘* (الهجوع) is sleeping at night, and it is said: a short sleep.

It is possible that *mā* (ما) is extra (زائدة) and **يَهْجَعُونَ** is the predicate of *kānū*, and **قَلِيلاً** is an adverb related to it, meaning "in a little time," or an attribute of an omitted absolute object, meaning "a little sleeping," and **مِنَ اللَّيْلِ** is related to "little." The meaning would be: they used to sleep for a little time of the night, or they used to sleep the night a little sleep.

It is also possible that it is a relative pronoun (موصولة) with the returning pronoun omitted, and **قَلِيلاً** is the predicate of *kānū* and the relative clause is its subject. The meaning: little was the part of the night in which they slept.

It is also possible that it is an infinitive particle (مصدرية), and the infinitive formed from it and its object is the subject of His saying **قَلِيلاً**, which is the predicate of **كانُوا**.

In any case, the "little of the night" is either taken in comparison to the total time of each night, implying that they sleep a little time of every night and pray most of it; or it is taken in comparison to the total number of nights, implying that they sleep in a few of the nights and stand for prayer in most of them; i.e., they do not miss the night prayer except in a few nights.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَبِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ** — "and at dawns they would plead for forgiveness." (51:18). That is, they ask Allah for forgiveness for their sins. It is said: the meaning of seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) is prayer (الصلاة), but it is as you see.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَفِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ** — "And in their wealth there was a share for the beggar and the deprived." (51:19). The two previous verses explain the specific nature of their conduct in relation to Allah, the Glorified, which is standing at night and seeking forgiveness at dawn. This verse explains the specific nature of their conduct in relation to people, which is giving to the beggar and the deprived.

Specifying the right of the beggar and the deprived as being "in their wealth"—even though if it were established, it would be established in all wealth—is proof that the meaning is that they see, through the purity of their primordial nature (الفطرة), that there is a right for these two in their wealth, so they act accordingly to spread mercy and prefer excellence.

The beggar (السائل) is the one who asks for a gift by manifesting need, and the deprived (المحروم) is the one who has been denied provision, whose effort in seeking it did not succeed, yet he does not ask out of self-respect (تعفف).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from Jamil from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً**. He said: "Ibn al-Kawwa’ asked the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) about **وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً**, and he said: 'The wind.' About **فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً**, he said: 'They are the clouds.' About **فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً**, he said: 'They are the ships.' And about **فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً**, he said: 'The angels.'"

I say: The hadith is also narrated through Sunni chains as in *Ruh al-Ma'ani*.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: ‘Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Sa'id ibn Mansur, al-Harith ibn Abi Usamah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn al-Anbari in *al-Masahif*, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman* narrated through various chains from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: **وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً**, he said: "The winds." **فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً**, he said: "The clouds." **فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً**, he said: "The ships." **فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً**, he said: "The angels."

In *al-Majma'*: Abu Ja'far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them) said: "It is not permissible for anyone to swear except by Allah the Exalted, but Allah swears by whatever He wills of His creation."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Mani' narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib that he was asked about His saying: **وَالسَّماءِ ذاتِ الْحُبُكِ**, and he said: "Possessing a beautiful creation."

I say: The like of it is narrated in *al-Majma'*, and its wording is: "It is said: possessing beauty and adornment: from Ali (peace be upon him)." And in *Jawami' al-Jami'*, its wording is: "And from Ali (peace be upon him): its beauty and its adornment."

In some reports: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي قَوْلٍ مُخْتَلِفٍ يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ**, it is applied to the *Wilayah* (الولاية) — *the divinely appointed authority and guardianship*.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **كانُوا قَلِيلاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ ما يَهْجَعُونَ**, it is said its meaning is: "Rarely did a night pass them by except that they prayed in it." This is what is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَبِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ**, Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "They used to seek forgiveness from Allah in the *Witr* prayer seventy times at dawn."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The end of the night for the night prayer (التهجد) is more beloved to me than its beginning, because Allah says: **وَبِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ**."

In it: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Umar from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying: **وَبِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ**, he said: "They pray."

I say: Perhaps the interpretation of seeking forgiveness as prayer is because the *Witr* prayer includes it, just as prayer is intended by "Quran" in His saying: **وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً** — "and the recital of dawn. Indeed the recital of dawn is witnessed." (17:78).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَفِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ**, he said: "The beggar (السائل) is the one who asks, and the deprived (المحروم) is the one who has been prevented from [the fruits of] his labor."

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain to Safwan al-Jammal from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "The deprived is the *muḥāraf*—the one who has been deprived of the labor of his hand in buying and selling."

He said: In another narration from Abu Ja'far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them), they said: "The deprived is the man who has no defect in his intellect, yet provision is not expanded for him, and he is a *muḥāraf*."
